msDesc.p: Notebook description: Cloth-bound hardcover notebook consisting of one thread-sewn signature of 148 pages with no pre-printing. The paper is (yellowed) white, but the signature is enclosed with one blue end paper, which constitutes the first leaf (pages 003_recto and 004_verso) and the last leaf (pages 0149_recto and 0150_verso) of the notebook. Blue paper is also affixed to the inside front and back cover (0002_verso and 0151_recto).
Relevant to publications: "Sea Unicorns and Land Unicorns" (1923).

Notebook Contents: MM takes notes from various sources and writes in different genres, sometimes within a single notebook. When a content type does not correspond with the genre of the notebook, it is coded in the margin as:

Edgar St. at Trinity Place and Greenwich St. is

A hundred foot alley east of Broadway running
North from First Street is called "Extra Place."
"The toad beneath the harrow knows
exactly where each tooth-point goes.
The butterfly upon the road
Preaches contentment to that toad."The epigraph to "Pagett, M. P.," a satirical poem by Rudyard Kipling.

Ode to Edison. Current Events
April 14—1930
Hush, little golden-rod,
Don't you cry—
You'll be a ba'loon tire News
By and by. Buffalo Evening ‸
by Carolyn Wells.
The Emily Emmins Papers.Wells, Carolyn.Emily Emmins Papers (New York and London: G. P. Putnam's Sons, 1907). October 3, 1914
An unpleasant personality of intense ego—a nonchalant
flipping of fringes in the eyes of those who would be courteous
a repudiating of social favors, despite desire to boast
about them. Nevertheless her subject—London—
cannot be marred. "The Englandish landscape though
of varying shades is green to the nth degree. It is a
saturated solution of green and sinks into the eye with
the sensation of indelibility."
"The Travers family; 'well born rather than well bred.'"
'Bbuses; "to go to Hampstead Heath you take a
Bovril; to go to the City take Carter's Ink; and to get
anywhere in a hurry jump on Horlick’s Malted Milk.
The jade cube. "It seemed inartistic to end so quickly
what I had long expected to find a difficult
quest."
Difficulty with the Eng. telephone. "Grawsp it stiffer,
Madame".
"First impressions of Piccadilly or Hyde Park are all
very well, but first impressions are incongruous in
connection with Westminster Abbey. What has crude
admiration to do with experienced sublimity?
How absurd to let the gaze of surprise rest upon age-
accustomed glory! What presumption to look at
ancient grandeur as at a novelty!"
"I am told the whole monument (Shakespeares) is wrong
in composition and in execution, but that is merely
'A fault to pardon in the drawing’s lines,—
Its body, so to speak; its soul is right.'"
Without leaving the United States, I could have found aTranscription from this book is continued at the beginning of this notebook.
Wells, Carolyn. The Emily Emmins Papers (see earlier note).more magnificent statue of S. in our own Library
of Congress, but no other representation has ever portrayed
the personality of S.—to my mind—as does the Abbey
monument."
"It is surprising to learn how many got into the Abbey
by the mere accident of dying while there was plenty
of room."
"The Abbey verger so dominates the group of tourists in
his charge, that they often show signs of almost human
intelligence."
formal
Afternoon tea in a ‸ garden with a sun-dial—roses,
a fountain,—ivy, and hills in the distance, is
"a Spangle of Existence."
"Salutation and Cat." Coleridge's, Southey's, and
Lamb's tavern.
No elevator from the 'bus top, so "we tumbled
down the back stairs, and were thrown off."
"No Londoner has ever been to the Tower. We used to
say we intended to go . . . now we don't even say that."
Newsboys hurtling through Picadilly Circus yelling
"Dily Mile?"
From the steps of the National Gallery take a long
look at the façade of St. Martin's-in-the-Field.
Trafalgar Square—and the National Gallery.
"Some pictures I should have loved but for their association
at home,
with crowded walls spaces ‸ and friends the "just love Art'."
Wells, Carolyn. The Emily Emmins Papers (see earlier note)."Where Pepys 'made mighty merry'."
Afternoon tea—sometimes of the "Glacial Period."
Guest: "I was found dead in my bed this morning"
"Really?"—(Again.) Ans.: "Only fancy!"
(again:) "Charmed! I'm sure."
Americans do not know how to give a Tea. We
conduct them in an amateurish, self-conscious
way, and with a brave endurance born of our
national do-or-die principles.
At Dorchester house amid much purely emotional
patriotism a 4th of July tea was served. Here a
sumptuous feast was served with the iron hand of
neatness and despatch hidden in the velvet glove of
suavity and elegant leisure.
"I don't know of anyone in London who would refuse a
fee, except a club servant or the King;—(and I've never
tried the King.)"
two
French money: multiply by 2; shift the decimal
point and you have dollars. If centimes, multiply
by two—decimal point again, and you have
centsime.
In a French victoria-like cab, Bella Wilfer
said, "Loll, ma, loll!"
An English Honeymoon
Anne H. WhartonWharton, Anne Hollingsworth. An English Honeymoon (Philadelphia and London: J. P. Lippincott Company, 1908).
Canterbury— "When that Aprillé with his showerés‸
The drought of March had piercéd to the roote."
Enter the Cathedral by way of Mercury Lane—once an
outer cemetery for the laity.
Christopher Marlowe: birthplace on George Street.
Notice a curious ornament on one of the angles of the
Chequers Inn. It was "the Black Prince's cognizance.
Go by Margaret Roper's house to King's Bridge
Here are headquarters of the Canterbury weavers.
The Black Prince is buried in Canterbury Cath.
His prayer "God defend the right."
"What shoulde he studie and make hymselven wood,
Upon a book in cloystre always to poure?" Chaucer.
Winchester Inn. "God Begot House".
Inscription _"William of Wykeham._
"Here, overthrown by death, lies William, surnamed
Wykeham."
The fan-tracery in wood on some of the vaultings was
designed by William himself.
Mary Tudor was married here. Sat in a chair
during ceremony.
St. Ursula = daughter of King of Wessex—(Winchester
was the capital.) The legend of the 11000 virgins
martyred with St. Ursula grew out of a mistake;
Wharton, Anne H. An English Honeymoon (see earlier note).an early copyist reading the entry "Ursula et
XI M. V. read M. to mean millia instead
of martyred.
Go to College of Seinte Marie. see "Trusty Servant.
Wykeham kept a "young scholar" from going
home at holiday time. Boy died of homesickness.
Under pillow was found "Domum, domum,
domum, dulce domum." 1680. The boys sing
this around a great tree—at close of summer
term. Go to St. Cross Monastery.
Salisbury. ?
Richard 1499
Warwick. Note the ‸ Beauchamp ‸ tomb in St. Mary's,
surrounded by relatives!
Earl of Leicester quite near. Lady Lettice his
second wife lies near. (Amy Robsart is buried
in chancel of St. Mary's Oxford.)
At "Lord Leicester's Hospital" his little son is
buried. His rich gown is highly decorated and
his poor little feet rest upon the muzzled bear
of the Beauchamps.
Richard Beauchamp—founder of Chapel—is
surrounded by 14 titled weepers.
Ambleside
In Dove Cottage, Wordsworth received Sir Walter
and Lady Scott, Christopher North, Lamb,
Samuel Rogers, Humphrey Davy, Miss
Wharton, Anne H. An English Honeymoon (see earlier note).Martineau—Coleridge, Southey, the
Arnolds—De Quincey:
Of the garden at Dove Cottage, Wordsworth wrote,
"Whose seclusion deep—Hath been friendly to industrious
hours." And— "If you listen, all is still,
Save a little neighboring rill,
That from out the rocky ground
Strikes a solitary sound."
Daffodils "dancing in the breeze"—belong to
Gowbarrow Park Ullswater
York. Bootham Bar—Mickle Gate.
Organ screen = English Kings from Wil. I to Henry VI.
The Jesse Window in the clere-story (Genealogy of our
Decora
Lord.) St. Mary's Abbey=Early English Archit
ecture
In the Guest Hall of the Abbey are relics of Roman
life in York. Inscription on the tomb of a child:
"To the Gods, to Manes, to a most innocent child,
who lived but ten months."
A Scotch toast:
Heres tae us.
Wha's like us? Name ava!
Wha's as guid? Dooms few."
At Windsor, see full-length portrait of Mary Q. of
Q.
Scots; also of Henrietta Maria
Near Cavendish Square are interesting spots: the
Church where Mrs. Browning was married, and 50
Wimpole St. where she had lived.
Wharton, Anne H. An English Honeymoon (see earlier note).Barry Cornwall on Harley Street
Romney No 32 Cavendish Sq. Sir Joshua
called him "the man in Cavendish Square.
To Marleybone Church, when Browning came
to London he always went, to kiss the
ground where her feet had rested.
Leaving this ch. take an omnibus down to
Piccadilly—and on by Trafalgar Sq. to the
Strand. St. Clement Danes is placed where
the Strand becomes Fleet Street. Here, the
river Fleet once flowed.
Gough's Square is near by. Here Dr. Johnson
nursed the lame the blind the sick—the
sorrowful. "Williams hates everybody; Levet
hates Desmoulins, and does not love William,
Desmoulins hates them both; Poll loves none of
them.
Go to Oxford by boat from London; leaving from
Wallingford; leaving the boat at Folly Bridge
and drive to Carfax and High. Get students'
lodgings; bedrooms opening into a living room.
Such places are entered sometimes through a
court that opens into a flower market; all very
rural yet within walking distance of all the colleges.
See the shrine of St. Frideswide whose nunnery dates
back to 740. (In Christ Church Cath.) See dining
hall of course, and go down into the kitchens built by
Wolsey. He intended to have the building taken
down, and a Glorious "Cardinals' Church"
Wharton, Anne H. An English Honeymoon (see earlier note).created in its place. Note portraits of him,
also of Henry VIII and of Elizabeth.
Oriel has
on roof
decoration ‸ that reminds one of Metzger.
Oriel men = Dr. Arnold, Bishop Butler
Keble the hymn-writer, Card. Newman, Thomas
Hughes, Bishop Wilberforce, Matthew Arnold
and Hugh Clough.
Rubaíyát is in the Bodleian
See reredos at All Souls'; and the Sir Joshua
window in chapel of New College.
The feet of hope scarce touch the earth.
See Shelley Memorial at University Col.
Go to Glastonbury Bath: frequented by Sir Joshua,
Dr. J., Boswell, and Horace Walpole.
In the carved ladders in West Front of Cath.
Epitaph for Lady Jane Waller.
"In graces great, in stature small,
As full of spirit, as void of gall,
Cheerfully grave, bounteously close,
Happy, and yet from envy free
Learned without pride, witty yet wise,
Reader this riddle read for me
Here the good Lady Waller lyes."
George Inn, 1456—Glastonbury.
Legend of Joseph of Arimathea. He and sons
and others—12 in all—went by sea to Marseilles
thence to Arles, across Gaul in 30 days; across
the Channel in 4 days to Cornwall=Lyonesse.
Joseph taught the tin miners how to extract
Wharton, Anne H. An English Honeymoon (see earlier note).the tin and purge it of its wolfram. Mr.
Baring-Gould says that to this day when the tin
is flashed the tinner shouts "Joseph was in the
tin trade." Here iare the remains of a church
whose foundations antedate by several centuries
the coming of Christianity to Kenkt. They say
that St. Joseph built his ch. of wattles (1 cent)
and St. Patrick came to Glaston. in the mid.
of the 5th c. and became its 1st abbott.
The Lady Chapel is built over the wattled ch.
of St. Joseph.
Go to the Vicars' Close at Wells, to "spend a
week away from time."
Ilfracombe—The Land of Lorna Doone.—
den
A Dene or Dane is a hollow place like a den.
Clovelly. Charles Kingsley brought his wife
to see it, saying "Now that you have seen
Clovelly you know what was the inspiration
of my life before I met you.
Camelford, some say was Camelot when
Arthur fought his last battle; (with Modred)
See the P. Office at Tintagel—mossgrown
eaves.
Old rhyme:
O green's forsaken
Angd Yellow's forsworn
But blue is the sweetest
Color that's worn.
Wharton, Anne H. An English Honeymoon (see earlier note). Exeter: 18 hundred years of unbroken history
Richard Doddridge Blackmore has a
window in the Cathedral.
February 15, 1915

"Letters from England,"Bancroft, Elizabeth Davis. Letters from England, 1846-1849 (New York: Charles Scribner's Sons, 1904). Mrs. George Bancroft.Bancroft, Elizabeth Davis [1803-1866]. Wife of American historian and statesman George Bancroft.
"I wish I were better prepared for my life here by a
more extended culture; mere fine ladyism will not
do, or prosy bluism, but one needs for a thorough
enjoyment of society, a healthy, practical, and
extensive culture, and a use of the modern languages
would be convenient."
February 20, 1915. English Hours. Henry James.James, Henry. English Hours (Boston and New York: Houghton, Mifflin and Company).
This book was read shortly after
the fire; in October.In October 1914 there was a fire in the newly renovated bathroom of the Moores' home in Carlisle.
"The sense of approach was already almost
intolerably strong at Liverpool, where, as I remember,
the perception of the English character of everything
was as acute as surprise—but It was expectation
exquisitely gratified, superabundantly confirmed.

l.add: one looked across the wintry ocean into the strange
dark, lonely freshness of the coast of Ireland, & then at

lg.l: Before we could come up to the city, ‸ —the black
steamers knocking about in the yellow Mersey,
under a sky so low that they seemed to touch
it with their funnels."
to be
"Morleys Hotel (London) proved ‸ a ruddy spot;
the coffee room fire—the hospitable mahogany, the sense
that in this stupendous city, this, at any rate for the hour
James, Henry. English Hours (see earlier note).was a shelter and a point of view.
My little bedroom-candle set in its deep basin, caused
the vast four-poster to project a huge shadow and
me to think of "The Ingoldsby Legends."
"The low, magnificent medium of the sky, where
the smoke and fog and the weather in general, the
strangely undefined hour of the day and season of the
year, the emanations of industries, the reflections
of furnaces, the red gleams and blurs that may or may
not be of sunset—as you never see any source of
radiance, you can't in the least tell—all hang
together in a complication, a shifting but irremoveable
canopy. They form the undertone of the deep perpetual
voice of the place."
"The absence of style, or of intention of style is the
most general characteristic of London. In Paris, the
idea of beautiful and stately arrangement has
never been out of fashion; the art of composition
has always been at work or at play. On the
banks of the Thames—it is a tremendous chapter
of accidents. But if there be not the intention,
there is at least the accident of style, which
appears to proceed from three great sources: one
the simple greatness—you may find yourself
in a shabby corner but it never occurs to you that this
is the end of it. Another is the atmosphere, with
magnificent mystifications, making everything
brown, rich, dim, vague, magnifies distances and
minimises details, confirms the inference of
The last is the congregation of the parks. They
spread with a luxury of space in the center of the
town and make a pastoral landscape under
James, Henry. English Hours (see earlier note).the smoky sky."
"The view from the bridge over the Serpentine
has an extraordinary nobleness.
"It takes London to put you in the way of a
rustic walk from Notting Hill to Whitehall.
You may travel this immense diagonal
altogether on soft, fine turf.
"Though we snatch what we desire,
(dare) in
We may not snatch it eagerly."As James mentions, these lines come from Matthew Arnold's poem "The Sick King in Bokhara." Said of
connection with
London manners. "She, (London) however, snatches
it more than eagerly if that be the only way she can
get it. She lives by notes—they are her very heart-beats;
but those that bear her signatures are as disjointed as
the ravings of delirium, and have nothing in
common with the epistolary art—but a postage
stamp."
Somerset House stands up on a pedestal.
The palace of Westminster reclines—on the big
parliamentary bench of its terrace. The
Embankment—does what it can, and the
mannered houses of Chelsea stare across
Battersea Park like 18th century ladies surveying
a horrid wilderness. * Charing Cross
R.R Station is a crime—Wildbank Prison, a
worse act of violence than any it was erected
to punish, and the waterside generally a
renunciation of effect. But its very cynicism
is expressive. We may as well let the case stand.
It doesn’t prevent my being more or less thrilled
by the journey on a penny-steamer to Greenwich.
Chester. Still Henry James.
James, Henry. English Hours (see earlier note).English Hours. 1872
"The walls—that ancient property—now lovingly
readopted and consecrated to modern amenity.
An hour's stroll will bring you back to your
starting point. Every few steps as you go, you
see some little court or alley boring toward it
through the close-pressed houses. It is full
of the crooked, the accidental, the un-foreseen."
Shop fronts: "I waste more time than I should care to
confess, in covetous contemplation of the vast
clear panes behind which the nether
garments of gentlemen are daintily suspended
from glittering brass rods. The Rows are as
scenic as you could wish—but the Cathedral:
The towers rise high enough to belong to the far-
off air that vibrates with the chimes and the
swallows." You directly become "conscious
of that sweet cool mustiness of in the air which
seems to haunt these places as the very climate of
Episcopacy
March 24. Dr. Norcross has gone—(March 8) I have
been ill—and Marianne made so by nursing.
The blooming tangle in Devon—
"Birds-eye and kings-finger and wandering sailor"
The cathedral at Exeter.
The secret of the impressiveness of such a Norman
tower, I cannot pretend to have discovered. It
lies largely in the look of having been proudly
James, Henry. English Hours (see earlier note).and sturdily built—as if the masons had
been urged by a trumpet-blast, and the stones
had been squared by thea battle-axe. A Greek
temple preserves a kind of fresh immortality in
its concentrated refinement, and a gothic
cathedral in its adventurous exuberance; but
but a Norman tower stands up like some simple
strong man in his might, bending a melancholly
brow upon an age that demands that strength
shall be cunning.
"At Ilfracombe—excellent beaches—and a sign-board
threatening legal pursuit if you attempt to evade the
mental
payment of the sacrificial penny.
"At Porlock the thatch seemed steeper and heavier,
cottage
the yellow roses on the ‸ walls more cunningly mated
with the crumbling stucco. I listened to the
"simple prattle of a blue-eyed old sexton who showed
me where as a boy he had scratched his name on
the recumbent lady's breast; (a crusading knight
and his lady).
"It may be said of the English, as is said of the council
of war in Sheridan's farce 'The Critic—that when
they do agree, their unanimity is wonderful.'
For one definite precedent in America, in England
there are fifty; (tennis playing, cricket, china
collecting, church going;)MM borrows from the previous four lines in "Sea Unicorns and Land Unicorns." the latter most striking
example of all. At Easter all the world disperses
for a week or ten days from London. The English
have a much higher relish for active leisure than
James, Henry. English Hours (see earlier note).we have. A large appetite for holidays,
the ability not only to take them but to use them
know what to do with them is the sign of a robust
people. Such holidays as we take—very
often too in Europe—are frequently a privilege
that lies rather heavy on our hands!
a charming jumble of
Canterbury: ‸ Norman arches and English
points and perpendiculars.
Astride of the roof sits a huge gothic
tower one of the latest portions of the cathedral
though it looks like one of the earliest.
Like the rest of the building it has a
magnificent colour — a sort of rich dull
yellow.
Rye: What is it that nourishes the sense of
obliterated history? The air is like that
of a room through which something has
been carried that you are aware of
without having seen.
March 25, 1915
(Tomorrow we go to Washington and to Mt. Vernon with Ben.)
May 21, 1915 "Oxford"Lang, Andrew. Oxford (London: Seeley, Service and Co. Ltd., 1922).—Andrew Lang.Lang, Andrew [1844-1912]. Scottish writer known for his collections of folk and fairy tales.
Speaking of the Renaissance coming to Oxford,
Lang says, "When the world ran after loveliness of
form and colour, as in other ages it has run after
war-like renown, or the far-off rewards of the saintly
life, what was Oxford doing?" And then he quotes
the saying—"The spring came slowly up this way."
"Politics, trade, law, sport, religion," says Lang,
Lang, Andrew. Oxford (see earlier note). will claim their own in England." He thinks
no amount of "University Commissions" will
change the nature of Englishmen; he says that
the 15th and 16th centuries—marked in Italy
by the Revival of Letters—"was in England
given over to dynastic squabbles, and to religious
broils. The Renaissance in England was like
a bitter and changeful spring. BrewerSee entries for "Renaissance" and "Renaissance Period" in Brewer's Dictionary of Phrase and Fable (1870). gives dates of
the Renaissance, as 1494
-1557 from
In art—Raphael, ancient
June 16— paintings exhumed; Leo X
Lang, Andrew. Oxford (see earlier note). Holborn is from Old Bourne; (bourne meaning river
Mary-le-bone is not a corruption of Maryie la bonne
bourne
but of Mary-on-the-river; ‸ meaning river.
June 21. "Oxford is bitterly historical." Consider the
fanaticism of Antony Wood, and of Bryan Twyne.
Lang says "most old towns are like palimpsests,
parchments which have been scrawled over again and
again, by their successive owners. The convenient
site among the interlacing waters of the Isis and
the Cherwell, has commended itself to men in
one age after another. Each generation has used it
for its own purpose, for war, for trade, for
learning, for religion; and war, trade, religion
and learning, have left on Oxford their peculiar
marks. No set of its occupants was very eager to deface
or destroy the buildings of its predecessors. Old
things were turned to new uses, or altered to
suit new tastes; they were not overthrown and
carted away.
"Grope Lane"! toward Merton.
Under the House of Lancaster, learning dwindled
though Colleges increased.
"The Fellows of Lincoln did not spare to ring a merry peal
Lang, Andrew. Oxford (see earlier note).on their gaudy-day, even though the Queen had just left
her bitter and weary life.”
arch bishop
See the gorgeous vestments in St. John’s College. (Laud’s)
Old Anthony Wood (historian of Oxford)—had been "done despite"
by Dick Peers his translator—and gave Dick many a bloody
nose and a black eye; but when Dick became pro-proctor,
Wood was
Wood was afraid to meet Peers, and "although ‸ a good bowzeing
blad" never dared be out after nine!!
Wood died in 1695 "forgiving every one." "He could well afford
to do so. In his Athenae Oxonienses he had written the
lives of all his enemies."
Prideaux says "St. Mary's is suffering restoration; and
"the old men—including Wood—exceedingly exclaim
against it."
The Turl Gate—a corruption of Thorold Gate
Dr. Johnson's room in Pembroke, was in the Gate
Tower—high up.
Gibbon says "Some duties", may possibly have been imposed
the poor
upon Oxford scholars, but the velvet cap was the cap of
liberty!" Gibbon himself was a gentleman commoner.
Dr. Johnson said of his own old tutor, "a man who
becomes Jorden's pupil, becomes his son."
Lang says to read Terrae Filius, "if you wish to see
a carping, cross-grained, scandal-loving, Whiggish
assailant of Alma Mater!" The 1st vol. of this bi-
weekly periodical was printed for R. Franklin,
under Tom's Coffee House in Russell St. Covent Garden.
Among the aversions of Humphrey Prideaux were
the "gentlemen of All Souls." "All Souls is a
Lang, Andrew. Oxford (see earlier note). scandalous place" says Prideaux after he has
related with seeming satisfaction that "Thr copies
of Marc Antonio's engravings after the drawings
which
of Giulio Romano ‸ had been secretly printed off
had been seased and the owners threatened with
expulsion!
Lang evidently has little sympathy with the modern
spirit at Oxford that has made it possible for
Fellows to marry. After looking at the matter from
however,
various points of view, he says ‸ "no doubt the 'society'
of Oxford is very much more charming and
vivacious than it used to be in the days when Tony Wood
was the surly champion of celibacy."
→ Why—is Magdalene College called "the daughter of New
College, and the Mother of Christ Church?

Literary Celebrities of the English Lakes. SessionsSessions, Frederick. Literary Celebrities of the English Lakes (London: Elliot Stock, 1905).
Quoting from Crabbe Robinson's Diary. "Hartley Coleridge
sang a comic song that kept me very grave."
Charles Lamb said of Charles Lloyd: "I'll think
less meanly of myself that Lloyd will
sometimes think ofn me."
John of Ghent = 4th son of Edward III. He is the
ancestor of all the 'monarchs who have succeeded
him.

July 26, 1916. The Inns of Court. Hyacinth RoseRingrose, Hyacinthe. The Inns of Court (London: Paul Musson, 1909).Ringrose, Hyacinthe [1872-1946]. English-born lawyer and author who practiced law in New York City.
"" " " " will always be sacred
monuments of the Common Law."
Ben Jonson names them "the noblest nurseries of Humanity
and Liberty in the kingdom."
Fleet Street—the journalistic center of modern London
From " " one can in a minute be in a mediaeval
Lang, Andrew. Oxford (see earlier note). London—in the Temple where are two of the four
ancient Inns of Court, Middle Temple, and
Inner Temple.
Lincoln’s Inn is in Chancery Lane.
Gray’s " " " Holborn.
Formerly there were nine Inns of Chancery,
subsidiary to these four, (above mentioned).
Clifford's, Clement's, Lyon's (now the site of the Globe
Theater) attached to the Inner Temple; New Inn, and
Strand Inn " " " " " ; FCarnival's Inn
and Thaires—(to Lincoln's Inn); Staple Inn and
Barnard's to Gray's Inn. Serjeant's Inn set apart
for Serjeants-at-law (name derived from
Fratres servientes of the Knights Templar.)
A gentil manciple was ther of the Temple,
Of whom achatours mighten take ensample,
For to be wise in bying of vitaille
For, whether that he paid or toke by taille,
Aldgate he waited so in his achate
That he was aye before in his estate.Chaucer, Geoffrey. "The Manciple's Tale" in The Canterbury Tales.
When Lamb lived in Middle Temple Lane, he
wrote (1810) "Hare Court rooms are delicious
—a pump always going, the water of which
is excellent cold,—with brandy—and not very
insipid without. The household gods are slow to
come, but here I mean to live and die." Come and
bring your friends."
The order of the Knights Templars estab. by Baldwin
King of Jerusalem 1118 to protect Christian pilgrims on
their way to Jerusalem, found a home in England 1128.
abolished in 1312 by Pope Clement V.
Lang, Andrew. Oxford (see earlier note). Lord Bacon did much to beautify the garden
in Gray's Inn. "It is the purest of human pleasures.
—the greatest refreshment to the spirit of man."
Charles Lamb calls the gardens of Gray's
Inn, "the best gardens of any in the Inns of Court,
my beloved temple not forgotten." (They are not
today what they were in old days.)
Pepys in May 1662—writes "Here I, to Grayes Inn
walk, all alone, and with great pleasure seeing the
fine ladies walk there." Again, "When church
was done, my wife and I walked to Graye's Inn
to observe fashions of the ladies, because of my
wife's making some clothes."
Bacon used 25 $ £. in two years laying out
walks and planting "privet, violets, primroses, and
vines." The total expenditure was for the Inn
and garden, was 489 £!
Staple Inn is so called because, anciently, it was an
exchange for wool merchants—staplers.
Coleridge in "Work without hope".
"Work without hope draws nectar in a sieve,
And hope without an object cannot live."

HymnProcter, Adelaide. Hymn 290 in Coffin, Henry Sloane and Vernon, Ambrose White. Hymns of the Kingdom of God, rev. ed. (New York: A. S. Barnes Company, 1916). by Adelaide Proctor.Procter, Adelaide Anne [1825-1864]. Popular English poet of the Victorian era.
1 My God I thank Thee, who hast made
The Earth so bright,
So full of splendor and of joy,
Beauty and light;
So many glorious things are here,
Noble and right.
2 I thank thee too, that Thou has made joy
Joy to abound,
So many gentle thoughts and deeds
Circling us round,
That in the darkest spot of earth
Some love is found.
3 I thank thee more that all our joy
Is touched with pain.
That shadows fall on brightest hours,
Procter, Adelaide. Hymn 290 in Hymns of the Kingdom of God (see earlier note).That thorns remain;
So that Earth's bliss may be our guide,
And not our chain.
4 For thou who knowest, Lord; how soon
Our weak heart clings,
Hast given us joys, tender and true,
Yet all with wings,
So that we see gleaming on high,
Diviner things.
5 I thank thee, Lord, that though has kept
The best in store;
We have enough, yet not too much
To long for more;
A yearning for a deeper peace
Not known before.
6 I thank thee, Lord, that here our souls,
Though amply blest,
Can never find, although they seek,
A perfect rest,
Nor ever shall, until they lean
On Jesus' breast."
Rudyard Kipling.Kipling, Rudyard. Hymn 484 in Sloane and Vernon, Hymns of the Kingdom of God (see earlier note).
wrote:
"Father in heaven who lovest all,
O help thy children when they call;
That they may build from age to age
An undefiléd heritage.
Kipling, Rudyard. Hymn 484 in Hymns of the Kingdom of God (see earlier note).(The same)
Teach us to bear the yoke in youth,
With steadfastness and careful truth;
That, in our time, Thy grace may give
The truth whereby the nations live.
Teach us to rule ourselves alway,
Controlled and cleanly night and day;
That we may bring, if need arise,
No maimed or worthless sacrifice.
Teach us to look in all our ends
On Thee for judge, and not our friends;
That we with tThee, may walk uncowed
By fear or favor of the crowd.
Teach us the strength that cannot seek,
By deed or thought, to hurt the weak;
That, under Thee, we may possess
Man’s strength to comfort man’s distress.
Teach us delight in simple things,
And mirth that has no bitter springs;
Forgiveness free of evil done,
And love to all men 'neath the sun.
Rudyard Kipling 1906

Edith Wharton.Wharton, Edith. See glossary. (In Mrs. De Boer'sPeggy and Paul de Boer were acquaintances of the Moores. Cosmopolitan)Wharton, Edith. "The Intellectual Honesty of the French." Cosmopolitan 66:5 (April 1919): 60-61, 94, 96-97; Wharton later published it in a series called French Ways and Their Meaning (1919).
"the general intellectual fearlessness of France"—
—not afraid of anything that concerns mankind—
neither of pleasure and mirth nor of exultations
and agonies.
The French having lived slowly have learned the
in
advantage of living deeply. In science, art, in
technical and industrial training, they know the
need of taking time and the wastefulness of
superficiality. French university education is a
long and stern process, but it produces minds
capable of more sustained effort and a larger
range of thought than our hasty doses of learning.
No race is so little addicted to fads—for fads
are untested propositions. The F. tendency is to
test every new theory, in the light of wide
knowledge and long experience. It is for
this reason that there are so few advertisements
in the F. newspapers. Nine-tenths of Eng. & Amer.
advertizing is based on the hope that some
one has found a way of curing some disease
or overcoming a common difficulty
more quickly than by the accepted methods.
The F. are too incredulous of short cuts to turn
to such promises hopefully. Their unshakable
intellectual honesty and their sound
Wharton, Edith. "The Intellectual Honesty of the French" (see earlier note).intellectual honesty lead them to distrust
any way but the straight and narrow
one when a difficulty is to be mastered or
an art acquired. They are, above all,
democratic in their conviction that every
yard of the Way to Wisdom has to be
travelled on foot, not spun over in a
breathless joy ride."[This last clause I
think poor; especially poor as a climax
or ending.] I now think it the very best.

Edmund Burke.Burke, Edmund [1729-1797]. Irish-born English statesman. 1790
Curates
‸ Seduced from their bishops by holding out to them

"In England we are said to learn manners at
second hand from your side of the water,
and that we dress our behavior in the
frippery of France. If so, we are still
in the old cut.
"Influenced by the inborn failings of my nature
and not being illumined by a single ray of
this new-sprung modern light—
Burke, Edmund. Reflections on the Revolution in France (see earlier note)."I hear, and I rejoice to hear, that the great
lady, the other object of the triumph, has borne
that day (one is interested that beings made
for suffering should suffer well) and that
she bears all the succeeding days, that she
bears the imprisonment of her husband, and
her own captivity, and the exile of her friends,
and the insulting adulation of addresses
and the whole weight of accumulated
wrongs, with a serene patience, in a
manner suited to her rank and race,
and becoming the offspring of a sovereign
distinguished for piety and courage; that
like her she has lofty sentiments; that she
feels with the dignity of a Roman matron;
that in the last extremity she will save
herself from the last disgrace, and that if
she must fall, she will fall by no ignoble hand.
It is now 16 or 17 yrs. since I saw
the queen of France, then the dauphiness,
at Versailles; and surely never lighted on
this orb, which she hardly seemed to touch,
a more delightful vision. I saw her
just above the horizon, glittering like the
morning-star, full of life and splendor
and joy. Little did I dream that when
she added titles of veneration to those of
enthusiastic, distant, respectful love, that
she should ever be obliged to carry the sharp
antidote against disgrace, concealed in
that bosom. . . . . . . . I thought 10 000
swords must have leaped from their scabbards
to avenge even a look that threatened her.
But the age of chivalry is gone. That of
Burke, Edmund. Reflections on the Revolution in France (see earlier note).sophisters, economists, and calculators has
succeeded; and the glory of Europe is extinguished
for ever. Never, nevermore shall we behold
that generous loyalty to rank and sex, that
proud submission, that dignified obedience,
that subordination of the heart, which kept
alive, even in servitude itself, the spirit
of an exhalted freedom. That unbought
grace of life, the cheap defence of nations,
the nurse of manly sentiment and
heroic enterprize, is gone. It is gone,
that sensibility of principle, that chastity
of honor, which felt a stain like a
wound, which inspired courage whilst
it mitigated ferocity, which ennobled
what it touched, and under which,
vice itself lost half its evil by losing
all its grossness.
"There ought to be a system of manners in
every nation which a well-formed mind
would be disposed to relish. To make
us love our country, our country ought to be
lovely.
"Kings will be tyrants from policy when subjects
are rebels from principle.

"Your affairs—etc. "If it be a panacea, we
do not want it.—we know the consequences
of unnecessary physic. If it be a plague
it is such a plague, that severe quarantine
ought to be established against it.
"The people of England can see a Bishop of
Durham or a Bishop of Winchester in possession
of 10 000 £ a year, and cannot conceive
why it is in worse hands than estates to the
like am't in the hands of this Earl or that
Squire.
"These atheistical fathers (French atheists)
have a bigotry of their own; and they have learnt
to talk against monks with the spirit of a monk.
Again, resources of intrigue are called in to
supply the defects of argument and wit. To
this system of literary monopoly was joined
an unremitting industry to blacken and
Burke, Edmund. Reflections on the Revolution in France (see earlier note).discredit in every way those who did not
hold to their faction. Nothing was wanted
but the power of carrying the intolerance
of the tongue and pen into a persecution
that would strike at property, liberty and life.
"Why is the estate of the duke de Rochefoucault
more sacred than that of the cardinal " ?
Can one hear of the proscription of such persons
and the confiscation of their effects without
indignation and horror? He is not a
man who does not feel such emotions on
such occasions.— He does not deserve
the name of free man who will not
express them. . . . When Harry the 8th
of England resolved to rob the abbies, as the
Jacobins have robbed all the ecclesiastics
he did not know what an effectual
instrument of despotism was to be found
in that grand magazine of offensive
weapons,—the rights of men.'
"A true politician always considers how
he shall make the most of the existing
materials of his country. A disposition
to preserve and an ability to improve,
taken together would be my standard of
a statesman.
The monks are lazy. Be it so. Suppose them
no otherwise employed than by singing in the choir.
They are as usefully employed, as those who neither
Burke, Edmund. Reflections on the Revolution in France (see earlier note).sing nor say. As usefully as those who
sing upon the stage. They are as usefully
employed as those who work from dawn
till dark in the innumerable servile,
degrading, unseemly, unmanly,
unwholesome, and pestiferous occupations,
to which, by the social economy many
wretches are doomed. . . . . . . .
Does not the sweat of the mason and the
carpenter, who toil in order to partake the sweat
of the peasant, flow as pleasantly and as
salubriously, in the construction and repair
of majestic edifices of religion, as in the
painted booths and sordid sties of vice
and luxury as on receptacles of transient
voluptuousness? Are the decorations of
temples an expenditure less worthy tha wise
man than laces and national cockades,
and petit maisons, and petit soupers,
and all the follies and fopperies in
which opulence sports away the burthen
of its superfluity?
Their purpose everywhere seems to have
been to evade and slip aside from difficulty.
Difficulty will not permit us to be superficial.
degenerate fondness for tricking short-cuts—
little fallacious facilities—
Commencing labours on a principle of sloth, the
difficulties which they have eluded, rather than escaped
meet them again in their course; multiply & thicken on
Burke, Edmund. Reflections on the Revolution in France (see earlier note).them; they are now involved, through a labyrinth
of confused detail, in an industry without
limit, and without direction.
By hating vices too much, they come to love
men too little.
They did not think of feeding France
from its own substance. A grand imagination
found in this flight of commerce something
to captivate. It was wherewithal to
dazzle the eye of an eagle. It was not
made to entice the smell of a mole
nuzzling and burying himself in his
mother earth, as yours is.

Hymn by John SterlingSterling, John. Hymn 11 in Coffin and Vernon, Hymns of the Kingdom of God (see earlier note). 1840
O Source divine and Life of all,
The Fount of being's wondrous sea!
Thy depth would every heart appall,
That saw not Love supreme in tThee.
We shrink before thy vast abyss,
Where worlds on worlds eternal brood:
We know thee truly but in this,—
That thou bestowest all our good.
And so, 'mid boundless time and space,
O grant us still in thee to dwell,
And through the ceaseless web to trace
Thy presence working all things well.
Sterling, John. Hymn 11 in Hymns of the Kingdom of God (see earlier note).Nor let Thou life's delightful play
Thy truth's transcendent vision hide;
Nor strength and gladness lead astray
From thee—our nature's only Guide.
Bestow on every joyous thrill
Thy deeper tone of reverent awe:
Make pure tThy children's erring will,
And teach their hearts to love Thy law.
Hymn by Thomas H. GillGill, Thomas H. Hymn 170 in Coffin and Vernon, Hymns of the Kingdom of God (see earlier note).—1881; written here
in the Spring of 1919—then erased (washed off)
and written more distinctly, May 5, 1923—
1 Each mighty power of evil, How doth the Lord assail?
'Gainst world and flesh and devil How doth the Lord prevail?
How doth the Strength supernal Come down into the fight?
How dost Thou, King eternal, Win victory for the right?
2 Some mighty man thou fillest With holy hate of wrong;
Some tender soul Thou thrillest With yearnings sweet & strong;
This woe he must diminish, This wrong he must o’erthrow,
This warfare he must finish, This evil power lay low.
3 The strength by Thee conferrèd To others he imparts;
The fire within him stirrèd Doth kindle other hearts;
By glowing souls attended He rushes on the foe;
The right is well defended, The evil power laid low.
4 That army, Lord, Thou leadest, That warfare Thou dost
share;
That victory Thou speedest, The Lord of hosts is there.
Then send the Spirit fervent, The fire that never fails;
To lighten each true servant, Until Thy cause
prevails
By Padraic Colum.
An Drinaun Donn.
(The Blossom of the Black-thorn)Colum, Padraic. "An Drinaun Donn." Wild Earth and Other Poems (Dublin: Maunsel & Co. Ltd., 1916): 38-39.
A hundred men think I am theirs when with them I
drink ale,
But their presence fades away from me, and their high
spirits fail,
When I think upon your converse kind by the meadow
and the linn,
And your form smoother than the silk on the Mountain
of O'Flynn.
Oh, Paddy, is it pain to you that I'm wasting night and
day,
And, Paddy is it grief to you that I'll soon be in the clay?
My first love with the winning mouth, my treasure
abide, you'll
Till the narrow coffin closes me, and the grass grows
through my side.
And Mary, Ever Virgin, where will I turn my head!
I know not where his house is built, nor where his fields
are spread.
Ah, kindly was the counsel that my kinsfolk gave to me,
"The hundred twists are in his heart, and the thousand
tricks has he."

— A song I like when I do climb bare hills—
'Tis all about a hawk,—
No bird that sits on rock or bough
Has such a front as thine;
No King that has made War his trade
Such conquest in his eyne!
I know thee rock-like on the rock
Where none can mark a shape;
I climb, but thou dost climb with wings,
And like a wish escape,
She said,
'And like a wish escape!
The Sea Bird to the Wave.
On and on,
O white brother!
Thunder does not daunt thee!
How thou movest!
By thine impulse—
With no wing!
Fairest thing
The wide sea shows me!
On and on
O white brother!
Art thou gone?
Christ the Comrade
Christ, by thine own darkened hour,

Ah, how long ago it is

For the poor body that I own
I could weep many a tear:
The hours have stolen flesh and bone,
And left a changeling here.
Four feeble bones are left to me,
And the basket of my breast,
And I am mean and ugly now
As the scald flung from the nest.
The briars drag me at the knee,
The brambles go within,
And often do I feel him turn,
The old man in my skin.
The strength is carded from my bones,
The swiftness drained from me.
And all the living thoughts I had
Are like far ships at sea!
"I Shall Not Die for Thee."
O woman, shapely as the swan,
On your account I shall not die:
The men you've slain—a trivial clan—
Were less than I.
I ask me shall I die for these—
For blossom-teeth and scarlet lips?
And shall the delicate swan shape
Bring me eclipse?
Colum, Padraic. Wild Earth (see earlier note).Those even brows, that hair like gold,
Those languorous tones, that virgin way—
The flowing limbs, the rounded heel—
Slight men betray!
Thy spirit keen through radiant mien,
The shining throat and smiling eye,
side like foam—
Thy little palm, thy shining eye
I cannot die!
O woman, shapely as the swan,
In a cunning house hard-reared was I:
O bosom white, O well-shaped palm,
I shall not die!
My love she is courteous and handsome and tall;
For wit and for behavior she's foremost of them all!
She says she is no ways bound, that with me she'll go
free,
But my love had too many lovers to have any love
for me!

The Shoreham infant, boy or girl, as soon
as it can toddle, assumes a nautical swagger
or rolling walk which would upset the
gravity of a judge or a bishop. It always
claims the upper hand of the Shoreham
adult, and is always made way for as
it struts along the crown of the causeway,
morally shoving its elders into the gutter,
before it can well stand—and that would
be enough of itself to make its native town worth a
visit."

A little kingdom I possess,
Where thoughts and feelings dwell;
And very hard I find the task
Of governing it well;
1 For passion tempts and troubles me,
A wayward will mis-leads,
And selfishness its shadows casts—
On all my will and deeds.
How can I learn to rule myself,
To be the child I should,
Honest and brave, nor ever tire,
2 Of trying to be good?
How can I keep a sunny soul
To shine along life's way?
How can I tune my little heart
To sweetly sing all day?
Dear Father, help me with the love
That casteth out my fear,
Teach me to lean on Thee, and feel

That no temptation is unseen,
No childish grief too small,

says light is pre-eminent in Vaughan's works

would have all men cultivate "interior
always
self-possession." He was ‸ true to the practical

with God, and not outward visions or

Flammonde.Robinson, Edward Arlington. "Flammonde." Rittenhouse, Jessie B., ed. The Second Book of Modern Verse (New York: Houghton Miffton, 1919).Robinson, Edward Arlington [1869-1935]. American poet.
The man Flammonde, from — — — where,
With firm address and foreign air,
With news of nations in his talk
And something royal in his walk,
With glint of iron in his eyes,
But never doubt, nor yet surprise,
Appeared, and stayed, and held his head
As one by kings accredited.
Erect, with his alert repose
About him, and about his clothes,
He pictured all tradition hears
Of what we owe to fifty years.
His cleansing heritage of taste
Paraded neither want nor waste;
And what he needed for his fee
To live, he borrowed graciously.
He never told us what he was,
Or what mischance, or other cause,
Had banished him from better days
To play the Prince of Castaways.
Meanwhile surpassing well
A part, for most, unplayable;
In fine, one pauses, half afraid
To say for certain that he played.
Moreover, many a malcontent
Robinson, E. A. "Flammonde" (see earlier note).He soothed and found munificent;
His courtesy beguiled and foiled
Suspicion that his years were soiled;
His mien distinguished any crowd,
His credit strengthened when he bowed;
And women young and old, were fond
Of looking at the man Flammonde.
stanza. *There was a woman in our town . . . . .
There were two citizens who fought
For years and years, and over nought;
They made life awkward for their friends,
And shortened their own dividends.
The man Flammonde said what was wrong
Should be made right; nor was it long
Before they were again in line,
And had each other in to dine.
" What was he when we came to sift—
" How much it was of him we met
We cannot know how much we learn
From those who never will return,
Until a flash of unforeseen
Remembrance falls on what has been.
We've etach a darkening hill to climb;
And this is why, from time to time
In Tilbury Town, we look beyond
Horizons for the man Flammonde.
_______________ Edward A. Robinson

Lindsay, Vachel. "The Flower of Mending." Rittenhouse, Jessie B., ed. The Second Book of Modern Verse (see earlier note).The Flower of Mending
When Dragon-fly would fix his wings,
When Snail would patch his house,
When moths have marred the overcoat
Of tender Mister Mouse,
The pretty creatures go with haste
To the sunlit blue-grass hills
Where the Flower of Mending yields the wax
And web to help their ills.
The hour their coats are waxed and webbed
They fall into a dream,
And when they wake, the ragged robes
Are joined without a seam.
My heart is but a dragon-fly
My heart is but a mouse,
My heart is but a haughty snail
In a little stony house.
Vachel LindsayLindsay, Vachel [1879-1931]. American poet.

Culture means sharing in such likings as have an
authoritative basis of one kind or another, and are thus
representative of what we call standards of taste.
Culture thus is an affair of the feelings—of "sensibilities"
Sensibilities may be trained (educated) but
they are capable of the sympathetic response we call
culture. Education may extend beyond culture
but culture is in a measure independent of education.
Not all education is cultivating, and not all culture is
a
educated. Each term covers more in certain way
than the other.
The admirer of Michel Angelo finds a new impressiveness
in real human presences, the afternoon has new
charms for the lovers of Claude Lorraine. Likewise the
nights and days have a new radiance for the student
of Dante, and the ocean new wonders for the intimate of
Byron and Swinburne, London and the Thames for the
familiar of Dickens.

G. K. Chesterton in Vanity Fair.
"The New Renascence."Chesterton, Gilbert Keith. "The New Renascence: Thoughts on the Structure of the Future: II. The Machine of Tomfoolery." Vanity Fair (February 1920).
Our tools cannot turn into toys. The masses
of men in modern cities are recklessly resolved
to amuse themselves. But I should say they
never amuse themselves at all. They are
amused—which is very nearly the opposite.
To amuse oneself is a mark of gaiety,
vitality and love of life. To be amused
is a mark of melancholoy, surrender
and a potentiality of suicide.
The former means that a man's own
thoughts are attractive, artistic, sayisfying;
the latter means that his thoughts are ugly,
unfruitful, and stale. The happiness of a
people is not to be judged by the amount
of fun provided for them. Fun can be
provided as food can be provided; by a
few big stores and shops. The happiness
of a people is to be judged by the fun that
the people provide. In healthier ages any
amount of fun was really provided by the
people and not merely for the people. It was
so in a vast multitude of songs, fairy-tales
and dances; and it was so even in the more
ornate and official business of the drama.
Men of the mediaeval guilds enacted in persons
the miracle plays, with all their highly colored
Chesterton, G. K. "The New Renascence" (see earlier note).symbolism of heaven and hell. I have fullest
sympathy with modern Trades-Unions; but
I cannot imagine a railway-porter feeling
comfortable in the costume of the
Archangel Gabriel; or even a plumber
getting the full delight out of being the
Devil. Yet it must have been a very pure
delight to be the Devil. The carpenter or
the cobbler who had the happiness of acting
Caiphas used to borrow a cope or chasuble
from the parish church; and I earnestly hope
that the Archbishop of Canterbury would now
lend his apron and gaiters to a dustman for
such a purpose. But the only point here, is
that numbers of ordinary poor people acted,
—as badly I daresay as Bottom the Weaver;
but I am not talking about art but amusement;
about people's amusing themselves—not
merely being amused. None will deny
that Bottom amused himself, even more
than he amused his audience. Certainly
that great man did not stoop merely to be
amused; indeed to amuse Bottom would
be a bold and almost blasphemous
undertaking, in which all the frolics of the
fairies failed. . . . . . . . . .
We make a joke mechanical, and therefore
melancholy. It is as unnatural to pay
men to joke as to pay men to laugh. We are
making new formalities out of old frivolities.
Chesterton, G. K. "The New Renascence" (see earlier note).We are not having the real fun of making
new frivolities out of old formalities.
And having touched on the example of
hide and seek, hockey and movies, I
shall pass to higher topics like politics.

When shame and darkness covered Him
and thee
What didst thou see
O thou great penitent of Calvary.
Housman, Laurence. Selected Poems (see earlier note).That thou couldst beg this boon as thy
reward
In anguish?—'When Thou comest to
Thy Kingdom, Lord,
Remember me!'
In that most darkest hour,
Of hatred born.
When Satan's power
Showed Love held up to scorn,
What way
To thee came stiength to pray?—
'Lord, when Thy Kingdom cometh unto'
Thee,
Remember me!'
Above thy head the darkness did not rend
To kindle thought;
No healing sign was shown wrought
About that death-place of earth's dying
Friend,
To tell beholders, 'This is not the end!'
Thou through the veil
Didst gaze
Into the centre of all Time’s amaze:
Thy tongue ere Easter greetings cried, 'All
Hail!'
O tongue, whereon such fires of faith were
found,
O most triumphant sound
Of prophecy that ever fell from man
Since Time began,
That, with its dying breath,
Housman, Laurence. Selected Poems (see earlier note).Hailed Life in death,
And named the Victor in the Victim
bound!
Therefore to thee,
Strong Saint, I make my plea:
Pray lest I tempt my fate!
Lest with a soul too dead,
Drawn down to my last bed,
I have not eyes to see
Beyond my own gross darkness cover-
ing me!
Too late, too late.
For my sad soul’s defence
Were death-bed penitence!
Only for thy clear soul that hour sufficed
To pierce the drift, and bid the darkness
lift;
All we like thieves have stolen our days
from Christ,
And think with late avowal that guilt to
shift,
But at the last can bring no recompensing
gift.
O Light in a dark place,
Show not too fair a light!
Lest thy strong suppliant face
Draw weaker souls to night;
And they, in their dark need,
Have no such light to plead.
Therefore my prayer to thee,
Housman, Laurence. Selected Poems (see earlier note).Pray thou for me.
Lest at the last
Self-knowledge hold me fast
To sins I see!
So, while I draw strong breath.
Pray lest I, brought to death.
Die, and fall short of thee.
(The Same author)
As The Flocks For The
Brooks
As the flocks for the brooks
As the river for the sea,
So mine eyes long for thy looks,
I for thee.
As the sun drinks up the dew,
As the fire burns up the coal,
So Love strikes through and through
To my soul.
When far, feel thou me near!
When heavy of heart, oh, haste:—
Drain me to death, for fear
Lest I waste!
Lest, betwixt alip and lip
Of loves that thirst and cry,
The cup of offering slip,
And I die!

Miss Edna Kenton in the N. Y. Tribune says of James
Quoting from James's own words respecting Tintoretto—
(James was 29 when he wrote these lines) "The face of a man who felt
he had given the world more than the world was ever likely
to repay. On one side the power, the passion, the
illusion of his art; on the other the mortal fatigue of his
spirit. The world's knowledge of him is so small that
that portrait throws a doubly precious light on his
personality; and when we wonder vainly what
manner of man he was and what were his
purposes, his faith and method, we may find
forcible assurance here that they were at any rate
his life—one of the most intellectually passionate
ever led.

Lubbock, Percy, ed. The Letters of Henry James (see earlier note).Henry James's Letters.
→ Introduction. Henry James was always
engaged in the poetic fashioning of experience,
turning his share of impressions into rounded
and lasting images. He once confessed
himself exhausted from the wear and tear
of discrimination. Looked at from without,
his life was uneventful, singularly
fortunate; within, it was a cycle of vivid
and incessant adventure.
It is the artist who takes over the deed,
from the doer, to give it back in the form,
in which it can be seen and measured
for the first time; without the brain that
is able to close round the loose unappro-
priated fact, and render all its aspects,
the fact itself does not exist for us.
Like the "poor sensitive gentleman Strether
in "The Ambassadors" even his regrets were not
wasted, but helped to swell the sum of life.

"The historic glory of America lies in the fact that it is
the one nation that was founded like a church. That is,
it was founded on a faith that could be stated as
a creed. The national character was not merely
summed up after it had existed; ait was defined
before it existed. Some would say that it never
existed, no matter how carefully it was defined;
but I think this is historically false." First paragraph.
a
Last paragraph: A democracy is happy in having a
fixed democratic creed; otherwise might forget that
it was ever intended to be democratic. And when
men forget their birth and baptism, they have
nothing except the folly of yesterday with which to
compare the madness of to-day." Jan. 27.

From Willie Benét'sBenét, William Rose [1886-1950]. American poet, literary critic, and editor of The Literary Review. paper "The Literary Review"—Jan. 22, 1921
Ham & Eggs[Unsigned editorial]. "Ham and Eggs." The Literary Review of The New York Evening Post (January 22, 1921). last ¶
BThe vigorous, the dynamic, the beautiful in American life
have seldom attained to the style which is their due.
Beauty of lower New York, clear towers above hazy
cliffs murmurous with life; beauty of the spruces on
quiet Appalachians; beauty of the interminable plains;
the
beauty of ‸ Californian June, white sunlight on gray rocks,
lifted arms of the sequoias. The aimless cheerful audacity
of energy in America. The eager struggle of Americans
to rise from the common to the fine. Hard living, hard
working, hard playing. Types of boys and girls as hardy
and happy as the Greeks. Opportunities for fulness of
living, for freedom of action, for escape from the city,
for escape from the country, for escape from one's self
that older countries reserve for their favored children.
Why are all these held back from American literature?
Or, surging in, are so often sugared, flowered,
stuffed with sentiments and made into poor pastry?
Why do we better with ham and eggs?
(March 6, 1921.) Warner quoted these lines to me the

"From Harding and Wilson." New York Times (May 29, 1921). The example of America must be a special example—
not merely of peace because it will not fight, but of peace
because peace is the healing and elevating principle
of the world and strife is not. There is such a thing
as a man being too proud to fight. There is such a
thing as a nation being so right that it does not
need to convince others by force that it is right."

The Exposition." (San Francisco—1915)The Sculpture and Mural Decorations of the Exposition. Calder, A. Stirling, N. A. (San Francisco: Paul Elder and Company, 1915. A. S. Calder. Calder, Alexander Stirling [1870-1945]. American sculptor; father of Alexander Calder, the modernist sculptor.
Keats.
When I have fears that I may cease to be
Before my pen has gleaned my teeming brain,
Before high-piled books, in charactry,
Hold like rich garners the full ripen'd grain;
When I behold, upon the nights' starred face,
Huge cloudy symbols of a high romance,
And think that I may never live to trace
Their shadows, with the magic hand of chance;
And when I feel, fair creature of an hour,
That I shall never look upon thee more,
Never have relish in the faery power
Of unreflecting love; then on the shore
Of the wide world I stand alone, and think
Till love and fame to nothingness do sink.
Keats

We hear a cry from those who are about—
And from within we hear a voice—
That drives us on to do, and do, and do."

For better or for worse these women have

Seventeenth-century England, where all of

Century England, where all of the and
America will not do badly to accept
their poetry now for better or for worse,
since by many a sign it is here to stay
awhile; almost certainly more it its sort
remains to be written and read. It is
independent, critical, and keen, a
product oftener of the faculties than of
the nerves and heart; it is feminine, it
is fearless; it is fresh.
Not that it is all alike. Marianne
Moore, one admits right away must
be taken for worse. She wedded wit,
but after divorces from beauty and
sense. Her manners are those of the
absurder coteries, her fastidiousness
is that of the insufferable high-brows.
She wrote some pieces for Alfred
Kreymborg's "Others" which made
that anthology difficult to take
seriously, and in the present
volume she quite smothers out an
occasional passage of distinction
with verbiage like this:

No source provided for the following text.No Bad Weather. Ruskin.
Sunshine is delicious
Rain is refreshing,
Wind braces up,
Snow is exhilarating.
There is really no such thing as bad weather, but

different kinds of good weather.The last sentence is from John Ruskin's The Art of England (1884) but the verse is not his. At some point in the early twentieth century a passage from Sir John Lubbock's The Use of Life (1894) in which the sentence is quoted is turned into an oft-cited but imaginary poem by Ruskin.

speaking of "those good old days" that shows not
since 1745-47 have there been any wealthy

sort of
for the crimes that are the temptations of poor people
March 15, 1922 March 9, 1922
Chesterton, G. K. "Our Note Book" (see earlier note).went on then and goes on now. The idea of
punishing a man as a public enemy has
become an impossibility, and the idea of taking
away the private wealth of a public man is
equally inconceivable, especially if he is a really
wealthy man. The horror of the pillory is
gone: Is it worse than prison? It is a more
democratic form of punishment. A man was not
only tried by his peers, but punished by his peers.
It was no idle distinction for he was sometimes
acquitted and applauded by his peers.—There was
nothing to prevent the populace from pelting him
with roses instead of rotten eggs.
The most abominal cruelties have always been
committed in complete privacy. If there has been
one respectable thing about the executioner, I think
it is the fact that he was called the public
executioner. I do no like his becoming the bearer
of the bow string, the secret messenger of a Sultan.
In mediaeval times a man could be put in
the pillory for what was then called fore-stalling,
and
and is now called making a corner. Or, he could
be hanged. Placid well-fed gentlemen
walk about Europe and America who boast
of having made corners. Suppose I were to
suggest that some of them should hang on the
gallows. It is no answer to say they have
not broken the law. Those who are powerful
enough to make the laws have do not need to
break it. in a different paper

June 22, 1922John BurroughsBurroughs, John [1837-1921]. American naturalist and writer. in "My Boyhood"Burroughs, John. My Boyhood (New York: Doubleday, Page, and Company, 1922).—
The geese presented a much more ragged and
sorry appearance after they had been fleeced than
did the sheep. It used to amuse me to see
them put their heads together and talk it over
and laugh and congratulate each other
over the victory they had just won! They had
got out of the hands of the enemy with only
the loss of a few feathers which they would
not want in warm weather. The goose is
the one inhabitant that cackles as loudly
and as cheerfully over a defeat as over
a victory. They are so complacent and
optimistic that it is a comfort to me to see
them about. The very silliness of the goose
is a lesson in wisdom. The pride of a
plucked gander makes one take courage.
To his son he writes "The discipline of
mathematics is not culture in the strict sense;
but the discipline that chastens the taste,
feeds the imagination, kindles the sympathies
Burroughs, John. My Boyhood (see earlier note).clarifies the reason, stirs the conscience, and
leads to self-knowledge and self-control,
is culture. This we get only from
literature."
Phrases that amused him were "that
will keep out the coarsest of the cold"—
Eugene Field said that when he pushed
an old hoop skirt into a broken window.
Also,—"The chips literally poured out of the
tree as though it had sprung a leak.
The Stone house at Riverby was not placed
right to get the winder sunshine., not
"You write a book on "Winter Sunshine"
said his son Julian, and you let none
in your house." Burroughs replied
that if he had had the winter sunshine
in his house, he might not have
written the book.
Of his father, Julian says: "There was
something very natural about him, a thin
white skin that bled easily at a scratch;
fine hair that grew well and was wavy;
a fine-grained fluid kind of body, like
the new growth of ferns or new shoots of
willows; . . . . When he was embarrassed
he stammered,
‸ he would force the words out, with a little
hesitation."
Burroughs, John. My Boyhood (see earlier note).Burroughs tells Julian that he must
work better over writing. "Put a spell
upon your reader. To do this, you
must go more into detail, and be more
absorbed, yourself."

The SpectatorUnsigned review of W. J. Todd, A Handbook of Wine: How to Buy, Serve, Store and Drink It (London: Jonathan Cape, 1922). The Spectator 129 (November 11, 1922). quotes: The drinking of the true wine-
drinker is contemplative. He sips, he meditates,
he sips again, he revolves the glass, he watches the
wine clinging to the side, he notes the colour,
holding it to the light; he would not desecrate
the occasion by drinking more. . . . . The craving
for the burning satisfaction of spirits is a different kind of
drinking altogether.
Dec 9. Friendly Tips to the Tripper in England.
By a Britisher. N. Y. Tribune Dec. 3, 1922—Unsigned article. "Friendly Tips to the Tripper in England." New York Tribune (December 3, 1922).
Take a bus at Hyde Park Corner, bowl down Picadilly
to the Circus, where young Eros balances on
the top of the fountain, . . . . . . pass down
Haymarket to Trafalgar Square, . . . . through the
Strand & Fleet Street, up Ludgate Hill & Cannon St.,
by . . . Bank of England and off through
white chapel to the East End. You will be on the
outskirts of Chinatown. At Barking get off
and wander. You will find an old church yet
with stones which have survived from the Druids's
day. Over a little tobacco ship—a little frame
building, you will come on the date 1307.
Go in a punt to Richmond—tea-baskets
etc., Get a permit from the Amer. Embassy
to see the crown jewels at the Tower.
Go to the Caledonian Market; forget
your best clothes—get into the oldest rags
you brought along; take a Fourteen bus from
Picadilly out toward Hornsey Rise and
tell the collector of fares to let you out at
Caledonian Road. Pewter, China, Chippendale
are for sale. To the silken-clad, prices are
in guineas; to the shabby,—shillings.
Afterward dine in Soho; you are on
"Friendly Tips to the Tripper in England" (see earlier note).the fringe of it when you are booking
along the Charing Cross Road.

Atlantic Monthly Nov. 1922Sherman, Stuart P. "The Point of View in American Criticism." The Atlantic Monthly 130 (November 1922).Sherman, Stuart Pratt [1881-1926]. American literary critic known for his resistance to modernism.
__Dickens pictured our democracy as brutal,
boiseterous, boastful, ignorant, and hypocritical.
__Carlyle. Our quantity of cotton, dollars, industry
and resources he believed to be almost
unspeakable; but says the country has
begotten 18 millions of the greatest bores the
world has ever seen. That is our fear in
history.
__Matthew Arnold more friendly far, to
our political institutions and to our social
organizations than Carlyle—says (of Carnegie)
and his friends
"They have no conception of the chief defect
of American life; namely, that it is so
dreadfully uninteresting.' This dulness, he
explained was due to the average man's quite
inadequate conception of the good life, which
did not go beyond being diligent in business
and serving the Lord—making money and
observing a narrow code of morality. We
displayed a self-satisfaction that is
'vulgarizing and retarding,' Nationally we
were boasters. 'The worst of it is that this
tall talk and self-glorification meets
with hardly any rebuke from sane criticism
over there.' He adds 'the New West promises
to beat in the game of brag even the stout
The Point of View in American Criticism, by / Stuart P. Sherman
Sherman, Stuart P. "Point of View" (see earlier note).champions I have been quoting.'
Now no Englishman will ever fathom the mystery
of Uncle Sam's boasting. The little boasts of men
like Lowell and Thomas Wentworth Higginson
and Brander Matthews are only Yankee
whistling, the turning of the trodden worm,
a decent pride in the presence of 'a certain
condescension in foreigners.'
The great civilizations of the world hitherto
have been integrated by their religions. By
religion I mean that which a man, in the depths
of his heart, really believes desirable and
praiseworthy.
The point of view which I advocate is not, as it
has been called, moralistic. It is essentially
religious. And the religion of an intelligent man
is not a principle of repression, any more
than it is a principle of release.
Does religion tend to assist the entire body of
the people toward the best human life of which
they are capable? Advance to this point of view,
and you leave behind you universal sympathy
universal cynicism, universal aestheticism,
and the dark bats of the Freudian cave.
You recover something of that high station
wh. Emerson displayed when he said: 'I am
primarily engaged to myself to be a public
servant of all the gods, to demonstrate to all
men, that there is intelligence and good-will
at the heart of things, and ever higher and
higher leadings'"
Stuart P. Sherman—
John Ruskin's Letters

to William Ward.Ward, William. John Ruskin's Letters to William Ward (Boston: Marshall Jones Company, 1922).Ward, William [1829-1908]. English student of John Ruskin; copyist and drawing instructor. Dec. 20, 1922
William Ward's father wrote to him, "What
is the difference between a tradesman and
a man of trade? Do not be a tradesman
but a man in trade. Man is always above
circumstance."
"What did you see at Hampton Court? I would
suggest your thoroughly investigating one fine
piece of painting or scuplture and writing
down all the sensations it occasions in you.
A superficial observer receives only a
superficial delight. Those who penetrate
into hidden beauty enjoy the substance
which lasts when the appearance is
removed."
At Twickenham, Ward rose before
sunrise for 12 months together,—his
bedroom faced East—to sketch the
effect.

It was a little stolen fire you held to me.
It was a brand from my neighbor's fire I knew,
An ember whose gift would have made her
hearth burn low—
It was never a light that could burn up straight
and true—
It was never for you to give, or me to know.

The Little Young Lambs.[Chalmers, Patrick R.] "The Little Young Lambs." Punch 164 (February 21, 1923).
In the fold
On the wold
There were little young lambs,
An' the wind blew so cold
They laid lee o' their dams,
An' a shepherd old man
He leaned over the cotes,
An' a lilt he began
With a flutter of notes,
The little young lambs all among;
[Chalmers, Patrick R.] "The Little Young Lambs" (see earlier note).Oh, he piped 'em a derry down derry,
he did,
Since they were so young.
An' they stirred
When they heard.
Did the little young lambs,
Then they hopped, most absurd.
From a-lee of their dams,
An' they jumped, an' they skipped
With tip-toppetty skips,
As the little tune tripped
From the reed at the lips
Of the crinkled old man o' the wold,
As he piped 'em a merry down derry,
he did,
Since he was so old.
For he blew
That he knew
Why the seasons went round,
An' why green the wheat grew
To his pipe's pretty sound;
An' why rain follows sun,
An' how sun follows rain,'
An' how everything’s done
To be started again,
Till the stars like ripe acorns shall fall;
An' he piped 'em his derry down derry,
he did,
Along of it all.
The Literary Review. April 7, 1923

The Birthday of a Middle aged ChildKilmer, Aline. "For the Birthday of a Middle-Aged Child." Life (May 10, 1923): 22.
Aline KilmerKilmer, Aline Murray [1888-1941]. Poet and children's book author.
I'm sorry you are wiser,
I'm sorry you are taller;
I liked you better foolish
And I liked you better smaller.
I'm sorry you have learning
And I hope you won't display it;
(But since this is your birthday
I suppose I mustn't say it.)
I liked you with your hair cut
Like a mediaeval page's,
And I hate to see your eyes change
From a seraph's to a sage's.
You are not half so beautiful
Since middle age befell you:
But since this is your birthday
I suppose I mustn't tell you.)
Life May 10, 1923

Christian, dost thou see them
On the holy ground,
How the hosts of darkness
Compass thee around?
Christian, up and smite them
Counting gain but loss;
Smite them by the merit
Of the holy cross.
Christian dost thou feel them,
How they work within,
Striving, tempting, luring,
Goading into sin?
Christian, never tremble,
Never be downcast,
Smite them, Christ is with thee,
Thou shalt win at last.
Christian, dost thou hear them,
How they speak thee fair?
Always fast and vigil?
Always watch and prayer?
Christian answer boldly,

Simonson, Lucy Clarke. "Nature and Human Nature" (see earlier note).A farmer and his wife separated because of a
quarrel respecting which of two holes a mouse
had run into. A reconciliation was effected,
when,—months afterward one of them remarked
"But you must admit I was right." Separation
this time was permanent, and the deserted
house became a laughing-stock. "Agree to differ,
resolve to love." "Who cares which hole the
mouse went down?
individual
To discover personal defects of character,
and to gain strength, use a system of
squeak-cards. The idea is taken from the
story of a farmer's setting a green hand to
grease a squeaking wagon wheel. The lad
visible
rubbed hard, every ‸ part of the wheel, but
when the wagon was used the squeak was
as loud as before. Then the farmer took
off the wheel and showed his man how to
put grease on the axle. A little grease
on the right spot. "Find the squeak and
grease it." Let each pupil who wishes to
write on a card his own weak points
as he sees them, and put the card in
the squeak box. If a child succeeds
in conquering a fault and reports later,
a red line is drawn through the squeak
on his card. Of course confidence is never
Simonson, Lucy Clarke. "Nature and Human Nature" (see earlier note).violated.—)
July 5, 1923 From Edwin E. SlossonSlosson, Edwin E. [1865-1929]. American chemist and popular science writer. in
"What Sugar Has done in 100 Years." The World's Work,

for MarchSlosson, Edwin E. "Science Remaking the World, V: The Influence of the Sugar Power in History." The World's Work 45:5 (March 1923). The title provided by MWM is from the cover of the periodical rather than the article itself.
Napoleon said: "The true conquests, the only conquests
that cost no regrets. are those achieved over ignorance."
But like territorial conquests, the conquests over
ignorance may curse the conqueror if they are
improperly employed.
But we must not stop the advance of knowledge for
fear of consequences. In the warfare of man with
nature there is no truce. Unless man can turn force
to his own advantage he will be crushed by it. Unless
he can establish his supremacy and superiority
over the world of nature, he will sink again to the
level of the brute. As Matthew Arnold puts it in
his Sonnet to an Independent
Preacher
Know, man hath all which Nature hath, but more,
And in that more lies all his hope of good.
Nature is cruel, man is sick of blood;
Nature is stubborn, man would fain adore:
Nature is fickle, man hath need of rest;
Nature forgives no debt, and fears no grave;
Man would be mild, and with safe conscience blest.
Man must begin, know this, where Nature ends:
Slosson, Edwin E. "The Influence of the Sugar Power in History" (see earlier note).Nature and man can never be fast friends,
Fool, if thou canst not pass her, rest her
slave."

The little Eye Hospital now falling to pieces was
built by George III. when Marleybone was "green fields
and babbling brooks. In its Publick Gardens a
Miss Trussler made and dispensed fruit tarts
with which she hoped to give equal satisfaction
"There is a House with Stairs" (see earlier note).as with rich cakes and almond cheese cakes."
In an announcement of hers, May 6, 1759 she says:
"The fruit will always be fresh gathered, having
great quantities in the garden; and none but
loaf sugar used, and finest Epping butter.

The mystic life may be described not inexactly

interior religion.
By the occultist we understand the disciple
of one or all of the secret arts.
The mystic has no concern with the
study of secret sciences. He does not work
on materials outside himself; but he
endeavors by a certain habit of mind &
the application of a defined rule of life,
to re-establish correspondence with the
Divine Nature from which, in his belief,
he originated, and to which his
return is only a question of time.
Plotinus said there subsists another

Christmas 1924. LiesStuddert Kennedy, G. A. Lies! (New York : George H. Doran, 1909).Studdert Kennedy, Geoffrey Anketell [1883-1929]. English Anglican priest; chaplain at the Western Front in First World War.
Love is a bad word. It means anything from
lust to loyalty, from utter filth (filth)
to faithful sacrifice.
The supreme power in the world is co-
operation. History is the tale of how
the Truth of Co-operation has fought
the falsehood of strife and competition
all down the ages, and how slowly
and painfully co-operation has won its
way dragging civilization behind it.
It must win because human competition
is self-destructive. The only good that
wars have done is to drive men into
closer unity. All that War did for the
German Empire (from 1864-'66, & 1870)
was to give her unity and co-operation.

Fosdick, Harry Emerson. "What Christian Liberals are Driving At" (see earlier note).These, I think are the three tests of effective
Christian liberalism: It springs from the
expansion and deepening of spiritual life; it
dwells in the great centers of affirmation, not
of denial; and it issues in constructive
statesmanship for the Kingdom.
The two chief aims of Christian liberals: To think
the great faiths of the Gospel through in contemporary
terms, and to harness the great dynamics of the
Gospel to contemporary tasks. If that be heresy
the orthodoxy will have to make the most of it. For
like a member of the Westminster Assembly long
ago, we are praying "O God we beseech Thee to
guide us aright, for we are very determined."

It is the storm flowing from west to east with numerous
shifts of wind that assaults vessels approaching our North
Atlantic Coast. J. Conrad says: "There are no North & South
winds of any account upon this earth. The North & South
winds are but small princes in the dynasties that make
peace and war upon the sea. They never assert themselves
upon a vast stage. In the polity of winds, as
among the tribes of the earth, the real struggle
lies between the East and West. The compelling
voice of the West Wind musters up to his
service all the might of the ocean."
