O God, the God of the spirits of all flesh, in whose
embrace all creatures live, in whatsoever world
or condition they be, we beseech Thee for her whose
whose name and dwelling place and every need Thou
knowest. Lord vouchsafe her light and rest, peace
and refreshment, joy and consolation, in Paradise,
in the companionship of saints, in the presence
of Christ, in the ample folds of Thy great love.
Grant that her life may unfold itself
in Thy sight, and find a sweet employment in
the spacious fields of eternity. If she hath
ever been hurt or maimed by any unhappy
word or deed of ours, we pray Thee of Thy
great pity to heal and restore her, that she may
serve Thee without hindrance.
Tell her, O gracious Lord, if it may be, how
much we love her, and miss her, and long to see
her again; and if there be ways in which she may
come, vouchsafe her to us as a guide and guard,
Griffiths, William. "A Prayer for a Friend Out of Sight" (see earlier note).and grant us a sense of her nearness, in such
degree as thy law permits. If in aught we can
minister to her peace, be pleased of Thy law to let
this be; and mercifully keep us from every act
which may deprive us of the sight of her as soon
as our trial-time is over, or mar the fullness
of our joy when the end of the day hath come.
Pardon, O gracious Lord and Father, whatsoever
is amiss in this, our prayer, and let Thy will
be done; for our will is blind and erring, but
Thine is able to do abundantly above all that we
ask or think; through Jesus Christ our Lord,
Amen.
Our Father, we pray that Thou will be graciously
near these friends as they send away the form
in which their loved one dwelt; and after that
is gone, may they find all this house radiant
and beautiful with her spiritual presence,
and the light and peace of the Spirit of God.Source of this paragraph, which is not part of the "Gladstone Prayer," not identified.

This paper will serve no party but the people;
it will be no organ of Republicanism but the organ
of truth; it will follow no causes but its conclusions;
will not support the Administration but criticize

Oct. 17, 1926. The Arts, Oct. 1926Barker, Virgil. "A Trans-Atlantic Chronicle, V: Aix-en-Province." The Arts 10:4 (October 1926): 217-227.Barker, Virgil [1890-1965]. American art critic and professor and European Editor of The Arts.
On a hill above Aix-en-Provence stands the
small and simple house which was the home of
a famous painter toward the end of his life.
From the only open window of the 2nd floor studio,
intentionally disarranged with unimportant
reminders of him, the view lies full south to the
close-packed buildings and the church towers,
which seem to turn away, not in contempt,
perhaps, but certainly in self-containment.
There is no need to waste indignation over
this repetition of such an old story. Aix
hardly owes gratitude to one who carried
her name to places and people to whom she
was and is indifferent. She can afford
to neglect Cézanne a while longer; her
nearer past is too different from all that he
represents and she is—perhaps rightly—
quite content to live on her memories as yet.
"Silent and discreet" the guide-book aptly
terms her, as if it were describing her attitude
towards this very person; also "aristocratic
and scholarly" which would account for the
first pair of adjectives and the attitude together.
The blunt high-tempered artist cannot be
Barker, Virgil. "A Trans-Atlantic Chronicle" (see earlier note).imagined to fit into the sort of life which must even
now be lived behind those stately portals flanked
with Pugets caryatids; . . . . . . . . . .
. . . . . —Another feather of this countryside
which he seems to have omitted—…—is the
olive tree. Yet the olive is one of the most
beautiful of all trees; the silvery multitudinousness
of its leaves and the coiling life-hold of its trunk,
all the associations of its intimate and manifold
meaning to mankind, make up an extreme
complexity of significance and charm to
the contemplative mind. But visually it
is the etcher's rather than the painter's tree;
the over-elaborate detail of its combined strength
and grace cannot be rendered in pigment
without a certain loss of constructive
simplicity. This would apply with particular
force to Cézanne's own technical method,
in which the visible brush-stroke of color
performs the double task of defining the form
itself and placing the object in its place
relation.
But all the rest of the Aix countryside Cézanne
has treasured up to that life beyond life.
which belongs to pictures as well as to books.
Th an immemorial sense of humanity, of
isolation and self-dependence. The gravity
and dignity of all this—the just scale of
relationship between man and nature, these
are the things which the master of Aix has
encompassed by the way, as it were,
Barker, Virgil. "A Trans-Atlantic Chronicle" (see earlier note).In his intensely singleminded effort after
"realization."
The road winds over another rise and
reveals Mont Sainte-Victorie erecting
itself with pallid magnificence into the
paling sky. Someone has written that this
is Cézanne's real monument; and in
this idea there is further satisfaction thatthat
is a monument which his birthplace
cannot possible refuse.

proper order"; Greece, the love of the beautiful
and the striving "only for things attainable in
the days of my youth"; China, "a pervasive
humanness;" Egypt, an obsession with
death, which taught resignation and
patience; and Ancient America, those
invaluable adjuncts to our own civilization,
rubber and cotton, corn and tobacco,
potatoes and strawberries.

In a story revived from 1904 Havelock EllisHavelock, Ellis [1859-1939]. English writer.
defines the Celtic Spirit as against the Nordic.
He discovers it in the literature of Ireland
and Wales and not in such fragments
as come from Scotch, Cornish & Breton
sources. It is preëminently present in the
Mabinogion. "Always there is presented to us
the remote as remote" never the endeavor of
the romantic artists to produce the remote
as present. The Celt longs for what Fiona
MacLeod calls the "rainbow-land" and
is always concerned with the beautiful
and the harmonious detail. Inventiveness,
quick sensibility, with an aptness to
embroider statements, or to lie are
qualities of the Celtic imagination,
where as the Nordic, always dominated
by emotion, is swept by tension and
pressure; is always highly charged,
realistic, without the fantastic prodigies
Unidentified review of Havelock Ellis (see earlier note).of the Celt. The Nordic literature denies
supernatural aid & rests on human
personalities; the supernatural is never
lacking in Celtic literature: Mr. Ellis
finds Nordic writing most typical in the
Icelandic Eddas and the Chanson de Roland.
. . . . . . Shakespeare is influenced by the
two spirits; Keats and Coleridge are dominated
by the Celtic influence. Tennyson, thoroughly
Nordic, was unable to make an airy
tapestry of the Arthurian legends, while
Spenser, Cetic, was able to accomplish
this in his Faerie Queen. Houghton Mifflin Co.

"Power of characterization is the superlative quality

l.add: of observation nor of imagination nor

He was charged with monotony,—but life is

not greatly caring which side won the game

London's River Start to Finish.Gorman, Herbert S. "London's River from Start to Finish." New York Times (February 6, 1927). F. V. Morley. Morley, Frank Vigor [1899-1980]. American mathematician with many literary friendships among modernist writers such as T. S. Eliot and Ezra Pound. The Times article is a review of Morley's book of vacation sketches.
This flowing current of water which carries ocean
liners at London, steamers at Oxford, sailboats at
Port Meadow, a horse-ferry at Bablock Hythe,
launches at Lechlade, rowboats at Cricklade,
canoes at Ashton Keynes, paper boats at
Somerford, beech leaves at Ewen, and
buttercup petals by Kemble, disappears in
the grass of the Cotswolds. It is wonderfully
quick to stop. The change is monotonous—
that is to say here you are sailing on the Thames
and there you are walking on the Cotswolds—
at no spot can you say this is the identical
and only source.
After we had passed Kelmscott Manor
House we approached the Queer Country . . . . a
mile below Tadpole Bridge you become as
if aware that for the past few minutes
something has been watching you. No
person—there is no lonelier spot in England;
and no animal—once past the brooding
herons, there is no living thing in sight.
But there remains the feeling of being
watched; at least that is the translation
of our sense of the influence of this strange region.
It is not the African feeling of impending
danger, nor the agony of Northern loneliness;
it is a feeling different in quality, yet as definite,
as disturbing. March 8, 1927

The Seven Wonders.This poem appears in the first (1870) and subsequent editions of Brewer's Dictionary of Phrase and Fable under "Seven Wonders."
The Pyramids first, which in Egypt
were laid;
Next Babylon's garden, for Amytis made;
Then Mausolus' tomb of affection and guilt;
Fourth the Temple of Dian, in Ephesus
built;
The Colossus of Rhodes, cast in brass, to
the sun;
Sixth, Jupiter's Statue, by Phidias done;
The Pharos of Egypt, last wonder of old,
Or Palace of Cyprus, cemented with gold.

Croce, Benedetto. An Autobiography (see earlier note). person the falsity of that pedagogic theory
which restricts education to the first
part of life.—the preface of the book—
and the truth of the opposite doctrine
which conceives the inner life as a
perpetual education, and knowledge
as the unity of knowing and learning.
To know and to have lost the power
of learning, to be educated and to be
unable still to improve one's education,
is to bring one's life to a standstill,
and the right name for that is not
life but death.

Feb. 10, 1928 Hardy, and Wil. Archer in ConversationArcher, William. Real Conversations (London: William Heinemann, 1904).Archer, William [1856-1924]. Scottish literary and theater critic.
in the year 19001
W. A. And the pessimist holds . . . . . that the principle
of evil is the stronger?
Mr. Hardy. No, I should not put it precisely
that way. For instance, people call me a
pessimist; and if it is pessimism to think,
with Sophocles, that "not to have been born
is best," then I do not reject the designation,
I never could understand why the word
"pessimism" should be such a red rag to
so many worthy people; and I believe
indeed that a good deal of the robustious
swaggering optimism of recent literature
is at bottom cowardly and insincere.
I do not see how we are to improve the
world by asservating that black is white,
or at least that black is but a necessary
contrast and foil, without which, white
would be white, no longer. But my pessimism,
if pessimism it be, does not involve the
assumption that the world is going to the
dogs. On the contrary, my practical philosophy
is distinctly meliorist. What are my books but
one plea against man's inhumanity to man—
—to woman—and to the lower animals . . . . .
When we have got rid of 1000 remediable ills,
it will be time enough to determine whether
Archer, William. Real Conversations (see earlier note).the ill that is irremediable, outweights the
good.
W. A. And you think that we are getting rid
of the remediable ills?
Mr. Hardy. Slowly but surely,—yes.

Thou hidden Love of God, whose height
Whose depth unfathomed, no man knows,
I see from far thy beauteous light,
Inly I sigh for Thy repose;
My heart is pained, nor can it be
At rest, till it finds rest in Thee.
'Tis mercy all, that Thou hast brought
My mind to seek her peace in Thee;
Yet, while I seek but find Thee not,
O when stihall all my wanderings end,
And all my steps to Thee-ward tend!
O Love Thy sovereign aid impart
To save me from low-thoughted care;
Chase this self-will through all my heart,
Through all its latent mazes there;
Make me Thy duteous child, that I,
Ceaseless, may "Abba, Father," cry.
Speak to my inmost soul, and say,
“I am thy Love, thy God, thy All.” Gerhard Tersteegen

'Time is but a shadow

4 We were busy in our efforts to beat the tide
when Mr James appeared from over the
sand dunes. There could be no mistaking
his deliberate gait; his thoughts always
appeared to be leading him forward into another
world, very, very remote. He stopped &
addressed me abruptly, a mannerism.
"Boy at what hour is the tide high?"
In about 2 hours' time, sir," I replied
"Say hence, boy, say hence."
A second later he was moving forward
again deep in thought, leaving behind
him a humiliated little boy. Later, on a
few rare occasions I spent some
happy intimate hours in his library,
He was most generous & helpful to any
whom he thought possessed the love of books.
That was hard and shallow.
This was novel and rich and urgent—
voluminously filling the mind.

l.add: reminds one of the Fountains abbey:

The Capuchins (Matteo had made himself a
rough habit with a pointed hood or capuce,
like that worn by the primitive friars) were not
mystics of the cloister. They labored among the
plague stricken. All laid stress on that mixed
life of action transfused by contemplation.

ing mother in a gloomy London house, "dressing like
a pew-opener, & dreaming like a saint."
("Miss Sanders does not possess the rare qualities
of the biographer who can present the realities
of a life that are mainly interior; that can be
made actual to the reader only by an extreme
combination of psychological insight & expressive
power. . . . . It leaves us in possession, not
as much of a biography as of materials from
which a bi. might be made.
Early poem. Goblin Market
"Currents & gooseberries
Bright-fire-like barberries,"
Figs to fill your mouth,
Citrons from the south."
Earthly Paradise of House to Home.
"Frogs and fat toads were there to hop or plod
and propagate in peace, an uncouth crew,
Where velvet headed rushes rustling nod.
And spill the morning dew.
all caterpillars throve beneath my rule,
with snails & slugs in corners out of sight.
I never marred the curious sudden stool
That perfects in a night."

If they are uncertain about God, let them resolve to
make ‸ by frequent repetition ‸ that prayer of Charles de Foucauld
which in the words of his biographer M. Bazin goes
straight up to Paradise: "Mon Dieu, si vous exister,
faites-le moi connaître." They will not have to wait
3 months. Or, if they believe in God but doubt the
efficacy of prayer, let them bring to him each small
difficulty of daily life, or ordeal, and ‸ ask for hHis help &
guidance. Afterward note the difference in result when
such matters have been prayed about & when not.
'Religion begins with an experiment & ends with an
experience.'

Apr. 17, 1931 "A Journal of my Journey to Paris in the Year" 1765.
By the Rev. William Cole.Cole, William. A Journal of My Journey to Paris in the Year 1765, edited by Francis Griffin Stokes (London: Constable and Co., 1931). whom his friend
Horace Walpole called "Goodman Frog." Mr. Cole was
a rector near Cambridge; very diligent in research,
very High Church, "a connoisseur in the matter of foods."
"Not the discipline, but the sweets of retirement attracted
him." He would "classify his collections in a place
where parish duties would not distract him, nor
the mists from the fens give him rheumatism.
But Paris streets are noisome, the Seine a noisome
nasty ditch, the people dirty—the Cemetery of
the Innocents a dreadful seat of infection.
He crossed the Channel twice without being sick;
returned to the fens to arrange his collections &
huddle over the fire, to get rid of his rheumatism.
& talk of the wicked man John Jacques Rousseau
and the anti-Christ Voltaire.
If we are a little, or a good deal, like "Goodman
Frog," let us turn the other way.
these days of "accelerated sight-seeing;" from "High Spots
of Sicily" by Arnold Wood.Wood, Arnold. High Spots of Sicily (New York: Sears Publishing Co., 1931). In the catacombs of
the Cappucini Convent "decorum is so completely
observed that the two sexes are places separately."

The editor quoteds Dr . Johnson's thought that
there is a hunger of the imagination which "preys
upon life", because it demands a deeper
reality than good sense can provide."—I

l.del: myself think imagination is so far out of
the reach of good sense that it draws but
little from that source. Imagination must
"prey upon life", and never be content; &
"good sense" may well offer what it can.

From The Spectator Jan. 24, 1931 Review of Hazlitt by R. Church—Church, Richard. "The Prince of Journalists." Review of Geoffrey Keynes, ed., Selected Essays of William Hazlitt (1930). The Spectator 146 (January 24, 1931): 118-119.
"The Prince of Journalists." IOf all our immortal friends and
masters, Hazlitt has, perhaps the most fertile mind. There
is nothing that does not interest him. This faculty
makes him a prince of journalists, & a lively agent
between the vulgar man, & the man of books. There
lies on reason for his great popularity amongst scholars: he
supplies their defficiencies in knowledge of men
and affairs, and takes them vicariously into markets
and cockpits & other haunts of the crowd.
He make a running commentary on the myriad
persons & events coming within range of his dark eyes.
The mind that guides their glances is displaying a
coincidental set of allusions from the world of
art and literature, so that his mortals walk
[vertical line] <!desc: lines 29-22, left margin!> Copied May 26, 1931
Church, Richard. "The Prince of Journalists" (see earlier note).enriched & enlivened by immortal comparisons.
. . . . . Reading an essay a day one gains from this
odd whimsical, perverse tortured, joyous
personality, an intimate acquaintance with the
great spirits of the early 19th century:— ‸ Coleridge,
Wordsworth, Napoleon, Mrs. Siddons, Burke;
. . . . . . a great wit possessin an inexhaustible
memory and a gift for the most apt (often
incorrect quotations.)
He is never at a loss—never tongue-tied or
pen bound. He is a magician & can say what
he likes on all subjects abstruse or concrete.
It is a miracle how he found the power
when English prose was still rather a
cathedral organ rather than a piano-forte.
De Quincey & Wordsworth, just as much
as Milton would have found difficulty
in giving us a street scene. The result would
have been a lumbering of the Ggods on the
grand courses of Parnassus. We know
how Stevenson "played the sedulous ape" to Hazlitt's
prose more than to that of any other stylist, & only
at the end of his laborious literary life
succeeded in rivalling its hardness, its
artlessness, its brilliance.
. . . . . . . . (Criticism) In Shakespearian
commentary Hazlitt walks alone among the
tragic & sinister mountains of Shakespeare's
impenetrable nature, where exquisite
flowers of laughter spring at the entrance to the
darkest caverns of the human soul. No
Church, Richard. "The Prince of Journalists" (see earlier note). wonder that Keats attending Hazlitt's lectures,
was put into a fever of ecstasy. It must
have been Hazlitt who inspired him to say "for
axioms in philosophy are not axions until
they are proved upon our pulses."
The world knows too well from his Liber Amoris
how in his love affairs & even in his friendships he
provoked nothing but cross-purposes & savage
recrimination. Poor Hazlitt incapable of reserve.
He warns his friends that if they confide
their hearts to him, the secret will be in print the
next morning.
This artist whose genius was hail-fellow, and
whose muse was the spirit of companionship,
never found ‸ love of woman or the friendship
of men. That was his tragedy. So great was
his love of art, however, that in spite of his
loneliness he died saying: "Well, I have had
a happy life."

that is hard to the point of impossibility, but has been

Pieter Bruegel The Elder. (The Arts Sep. 1926)
Virgil Barker.Barker, Virgil. "Pieter Bruegel the Elder: A Study of His Paintings." The Arts 10:3 (October 1926): 129-185.
"Bruegel's minute accuracy of drawing
expresses his love for the individual as such;
his great masses of people express his desire
to see life largely & as an interwoven whole.
Moreover, the device of making the ostensible
subject of a picture an almost invisible
incident in its is an expression of an
idea as to the relative importance of the
individual and what happens to him."
G. K. Chesteron in The Ill. London News, May 30,Chesterton, G. K. "Our Note Book." The Illustrated London News 178 (May 30, 1931): 902.
I do not object to experiments as such . . . . Mr. Sitwell
has as much right to talk about a hairy cloud, as
an old poet to talk about a fleecy cloud; as
much right to do what he can with the hair of
a cloud, as the other with the hair of a comet.
But something much deeper and more mysterious
is involved. The old poets had a power of mixing
with their fleecy clouds & hairy poets some
ancestral magic of the nature of music; by
which even the quaintest of Cavalier conceits,
or the most newly coined of Renaissance
Latinisms, came weighted with harmonies
and a historic richness that prevented them
[vertical line] <!desc: lines 05-01, left margin!>May 26, 1931
Chesterton, G. K. "Our Note Book" (see earlier note).from being crude, even when they were new.
It seems to me that the new poets do not try to
recover that ancient wedding of sound & sense.
Some of them seem to have only passed from the
old Swinburnian phase of sound without sense
to the latest phase of nonsense without sound.

Albert Einstein. An appreciation by A. Reiser
lg.note: [Unsigned]. "Almost More Happiness Than One Man Can Bear." Review of Anton Reiser, Albert Einstein: A Biographical Portrait (1930). The Illustrated London News (July 4, 1931): 32, 44.
lg.l: Einstein dislikes public contention—he "suspects
superfluity of contention as an approach to truth,
which needs no help." The greater our brotherly
love, the more susceptible the human heart to
the need and suffering of the individual & the
masses, the more creative is the human mind
in all its divisions." Einstein has a hearty laugh
& a sympathetic understanding for everything
but he finds a too-obstrusive friendship more
unbearable than hostility. The noisey distasteful
worship which endeavors to turn the personality
of a scientist into a sensation." "I hope this is
right, the result would be lovely," he was overheard
to murmur in an experiment.

On the road through Ombersley, "precious village
Eberlein, Harold D. Little Known England (see earlier note). of black & white as you come from
Worcester—Masefield's "land of heart's desire"
gorgeous in autumn; winter in dull browns &
glaucous in frost; vivid mistletoe, laborers
mediaeval enough to be illustrations of Piers
Plowman. Bridgenorth is aloft on red
cliffs; a dramatic sight. See High Street, &
perhaps go to Tthe Swan Hotel. See the Stoneway
Steps St. Mary Steps, among other steps.
Also, there is a funicular railway; and
there are cliff dwellers whose forebears
burrowed back into the sandstone, in "a
time to which the memory of man runneth
not back," Richard Baxter dedicated his
Saints' Everlasting Rest to the people of
Bridgenorth. Bishop Percy s (Reliques of
Ancient Poetry) lived here. His house is now
Boy Scouts Head Quarters. See Rock House,
and Broseley; Long Mynd*& Church
Stretton. Ludlow. Stay at The Feathers, or
tThe Angel. See Acton Burnell Castle 13th Cent.
Stokesay Castle too—both each a fortifieed
Manor house. Ancient cedars of Lebanon.
Says John Leland: The Towne of Shrewsbury
standeth on a Rocky Hill of Stone of a sadde
redde Earth, and Leverne so girdeth on
all the Ttowne that, saving a little Peice,
it were an Isle.
John Leland says of Bury St. Edmonds, "City more
neatly seated, the sun never saw, so curiously doth
it hang upon a gentle descent, with a little river
upon the east side (the Lark); nor a monastery
Eberlein, Harold D. Little Known England (see earlier note).more noble, so many gates it has, some whereof are
brass l) so many towers, & a church, than which

Defoe lived at Bury, & wrote of it "the town
was crowded with nobility & gentry, and as the
town invites, so is the appearance of pleasure
upon the very situation; & they that live at Bury
are supposed to live there for the sake of it."
"The beauty of the town consists in the number of
gentry who dwell in, and near it, the polite
conversation among them, the affluence and
plenty they live in, the sweet air they breathe
itn and the pleasant country they have to go
abroad in."

3 0 the two ruling devotions of one of the most
lovable of men . . . first of all the quality
of his affection to his friends. (large correspondence)
He loved to be cherished in similar fashion
and to be held in remembrance, speaking of
himself as "singularly accessible to "demonstrations
of regard" and thanking heaven that "for him
remembrance was a great romance." (E. F. Benson.)
The second ruling devotion, E. F. Benson
tells us was his devotion to his art. To both
H. J. gave all the powers of his intellect. To
both, loyalty and service were unswerving.
He told E. F. B. that "Art should be as hard
as nails. He could not be other than "ferociously
literary", "corrosively critical;" no "facile
composition" for him. "Hew out a style"
he once wrote to E. F. B. (advice not taken?)
and in A. B. "I like verses that are like little
strange, cold pale green pieces of Chinese jade,
worked inconceivably." In same letter he gsays
"this mere attempt at a decent rejoinder
acknowledgement would ill-brook a word
of rejoinder." "To come & dine with you wd be
a rare romance. I shake you by the hand
and am constantly yours. H. J.
Benson, E. F. Henry James: Letters to A. C. Benson and Auguste Monod (see earlier note).In your so romantic home you give so much
more than you can receive—yet what
is "being friends," but that undistinguishable
obligation is law? Today I find myself
where the diff. btwn. sadness & cheer,
interest & detachment lies behind in the
road.
"What a block burden of a postman's pack
you must carry. Let me not add to it, &
only accommodate your shoulders ‸ to the
affectionate touch theof this illegible hand.
"You think too kindly of Roderick Hudson,
and I am very glad you do. Faint & far and
feeble it seems. But one's youth was in itself a
merit—it worked for one. I am yours with
distinct emotion
emotion. H. J.
"The thing I shall still spare you is the
smallest rejoinder to this.—The only thing I
wish to remark is that I do hold you faster
than ever. You will answer vive voce and forget
in the meantime Yours Constantly H. J.
"To left you alone in these past over-
crowded weeks is to be thought well of.
"We are both victims of our devouring age,
. . . How could I not enjoy the bland beauty of

courtesy and kindness in particular of
your parents? I carried away the same fine
old mellow picture as before; the one snake
in the grass, that I got almost no talk with
you"
"be of good & uplifted heart."
"a grubby hood near Rye."
"Don't for the love you bear me, open my wanton
little volume—a bundle of very dry sticks—
until you are hard bested beset to kinde
the fire of fancy. I mean, read it, if at all,
in a tram or a salon de lecture. That's the
drawback of sending such tokens as one's
books are: one means it as a mere affection—
gesture, a remembering nod—and it has,
yet, a vile appearance of of imposing a
time-consuming act. The thing is, in a word,
just a pat you on theyour back. . . . .
Farewell, fine ornament of a mighty order.
I shall look out for you in the courts of
Heaven—you will know me by a battered
Humber. (bicycle)
"You must feel, this X'mas, as if the side of
the house, the front of the house, of your life had
been blown off and you were all open the
elements. I don't know where you are, but I
Benson, E. F. Henry James: Letters to A. C. Benson and Auguste Monod (see earlier note).pray my sympathy may reach you. You are
buried beneath your occupations, I do know,
and I hope there is warmth in that. Also that
your Mother is in a climate and in kind
company. I think of the great fires at
Addington, and the ghostly slopes in the
winter dusk. What good things you
have to remember! Don't let me drop
out of them.—if I am a good thing—and
believe me yours always.
"I was prepared to inflict a visit; was
prepared to inflict a message, but I had
no intention of inflicting a letter.
. . . . . . I go on admiring you at a distance
and still more conscious of the scale
on which you are constructed, when,
out of your abyss, you can toss up such
a flower of friendship to yours evermore
H. J.
"My Remingtonese. The sad fact (only
it is not a sad fact at all, but a glad one
and I pretend to sorrow only for the
vain show of it,) I am reduced to that
or eternal silence. . . . .
You tell me more of your apple-cheeked
landlord, than of his lily-cheeked tenant.
However, I admit the personage does sound a
a quaint mixture—more than that—a
mediaeval saint; save that your friend only
cleans his own bicycle, where as the M. S.

When will the day come for any easy converse?
I respect your perpetual pressure and never
pull your sleeve. Some day I shall have my
reward, but it will not be in this world.
Goodnight, with every good wish for your
endurance of the great Christmas grimace.
I call it that out of the abyss of my wail for

"Never laugh at us Celts!" said E‸rnest Renan
himself a Breton;—"we shall not build the
Parthenon—marble is not our affair. But we
know how to seize upon the heart & soul; we
have a power of peircing, which belongs only
to us. We plunge our hands into the entrails of
of man & bring them out; like the witches in
Macbeth, full of the secrets of the infinite. We
have no turn for practical life, for chaffering
& bargaining. We are difficult to move. We
die if you tear us from home. In the heart
of our race there is a perpetual spring . . . .
Fairy land is our domain, the only fairyland
that only pure lips & faithful hearts can enter."
From Emily Bronteë.
Silence is the house; all are laid asleep:
One alone looks out o'er the snow-wreaths deep,
Watching every cloud, dreading every breeze
That whirls the wildering drift, and bends
the groaning trees.
Burn then little lamp; glimmer straight & clear.—
Hush! a rustling wing stirs, methinks the air;
He for whom I wait thus ever comes to me;
Strange Power! I trust thy might; trust thou my con-
stancy."
"dared beyond strength, hazarded
beyond judgment!"
